threats.title: Registry onlyVaultMaintainer Modifier Uses OR Instead of AND, Bricking Vault Administration
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyVaultMaintainer modifier reverts when `(msg.sender != maintainer || !hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender))`, requiring the caller to be BOTH the vault maintainer AND hold EMERGENCY_ROLE simultaneously. Since the maintainer is intended to be a timelock and emergency is a cold wallet, no single address satisfies both conditions. Functions addConnector, removeTrustedPosition, and updateConnectorTrustedTokens become permanently inaccessible to their intended callers, bricking vault configuration.
threats.investigation_rationale: The onlyVaultMaintainerWithoutTimeLock and onlyVaultGoverner modifiers correctly use AND (&&) in the revert condition. onlyVaultMaintainer uses OR (||), creating an inconsistency. This is directly observable: the maintainer timelock will fail to call addConnector on any vault.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != vaults[_vaultId].maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false) revert — uses || (OR) instead of && (AND) as in other modifiers

preconditions: Vault maintainer address != EMERGENCY_ROLE holder (standard deployment assumption)
Vault exists in registry

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Maintainer timelock cannot add connectors, update trusted tokens, or remove trusted positions without also holding EMERGENCY_ROLE
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: All vault configuration changes through these three functions are permanently blocked for the intended caller
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — affects all vaults immediately upon deployment

operational_questions: Are the maintainer and emergency roles ever assigned to the same address in production?
Can the EMERGENCY_ROLE holder perform these operations as a workaround?
validation_requirements: Call addConnector from maintainer address without EMERGENCY_ROLE and confirm revert
Compare modifier logic with onlyVaultGoverner which correctly uses &&

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe that vault maintainer (timelock) cannot call addConnector, removeTrustedPosition, or updateConnectorTrustedTokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Vault becomes unable to add new connectors or update token trust lists
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Protocol operations are frozen for vault administration functions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Only addresses with BOTH maintainer role AND EMERGENCY_ROLE can bypass, which is never the intended configuration
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — this is a latent code defect

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete inability to configure vault connectors and trusted tokens
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Vault cannot integrate new DeFi protocols or update trust settings

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: None — bug manifests during normal operations
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None required to trigger; basic Solidity to verify

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed transactions from maintainer calling addConnector
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Vault configuration stuck, no new connectors added

threats.title: Holding Position Swap-and-Pop Uses Wrong Key, Corrupting isPositionUsed Mapping
threats.hypothesis_description: When removing a holding position, the swap-and-pop logic reconstructs the moved position's key using `calculatorConnector` (HoldingPI field 0), but the original key was created using `msg.sender` (the calling connector). For token positions (type 0), calculatorConnector is the accountingManager while the creator is a connector, causing key divergence. After any removal that triggers swap-and-pop, the isPositionUsed mapping becomes corrupted: the moved position gets a stale entry, lookups return 0 (not found), and duplicate positions can be created, eventually exhausting maxNumHoldingPositions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The key construction on line creating holdingPositionId uses msg.sender, while the swap reconstruction in the removal branch uses calculatorConnector. For type 0 token positions, these addresses differ (accountingManager vs connector address).

proof_of_signal.pattern: Creation: keccak256(abi.encode(msg.sender, _positionId, _data)); Swap-pop: keccak256(abi.encode(vault.holdingPositions[positionIndex].calculatorConnector, ...)) — msg.sender != calculatorConnector for token positions

preconditions: Vault has multiple holding positions including token positions (type 0)
A removal triggers swap-and-pop (removed position is not last in array)
Token position's calculatorConnector (accountingManager) differs from creating connector

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: After removing any non-last holding position, token positions that get swapped have corrupted index mapping
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Normal keeper operations (token swaps updating registry) trigger this
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every position removal that causes swap-and-pop of a token position

operational_questions: How frequently do position removals trigger swap-and-pop in practice?
Does the corruption lead to duplicate position creation?
Can maxNumHoldingPositions be exhausted via duplicates?
validation_requirements: Create a token position from connector A, then remove a different position causing swap-and-pop
Verify getHoldingPositionIndex returns 0 for the moved token position
Attempt to add the same token position again and verify duplicate creation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Observe vault with multiple holding positions including token positions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Remove a non-last position to trigger swap-and-pop of a token position
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Corrupted mapping allows duplicate position entries
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeated operations exhaust maxNumHoldingPositions, blocking all new positions
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Normal vault operations trigger this — no attacker coordination needed

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Positions may become unremovable; maxNumHoldingPositions exhaustion blocks all operations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain fix — requires contract redeployment
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All connector position management, TVL calculation accuracy

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: None — normal operations trigger the bug

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: getHoldingPositionIndex returning 0 for positions that exist in the array
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Duplicate holding positions appearing in array

threats.title: TVL Underflow Reverts in Multiple Connectors Cause Vault-Wide DoS
threats.hypothesis_description: SiloConnector, PrismaConnector, DolomiteConnector, CompoundConnector, and AaveConnector all compute TVL as `totalCollateral - totalDebt` without checking for underflow. In Solidity 0.8.x, if any lending position becomes underwater (debt > collateral due to interest accrual, liquidation, or price drops), the subtraction reverts. Since TVLHelper.getTVL iterates ALL positions, a single underwater position blocks the entire vault's TVL calculation, freezing all deposits, withdrawals, and fee calculations.
threats.investigation_rationale: Each affected connector has an unchecked subtraction: SiloConnector `tvl = totalDepositAmount - totalBAmount`, PrismaConnector `return _getValue(...collateral...) - _getValue(...debt...)`, DolomiteConnector `return totalCollateral - totalDebt`, CompoundConnector `uint256 balance = positiveBalance - negativeBalance`, AaveConnector `uint256 poolBaseAmount = totalCollateralBase - totalDebtBase`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: tvl = totalDepositAmount - totalBAmount; — reverts if totalBAmount > totalDepositAmount in Solidity 0.8.x

preconditions: At least one lending position exists in any of the affected connectors
Market conditions cause debt to exceed collateral (price drop, interest accrual, partial liquidation)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Any underwater position causes TVLHelper.getTVL to revert for the entire vault
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin action needed — market conditions alone trigger this
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any market downturn or interest rate spike that makes a position underwater

operational_questions: What market conditions could cause each connector's position to go underwater?
Is there a mechanism to remove underwater positions before TVL calculation?
Can the vault operate at all when TVL calculation is blocked?
validation_requirements: Simulate a position where debt > collateral in each connector
Verify TVLHelper.getTVL reverts
Confirm all deposit/withdrawal operations are blocked

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify a vault with lending positions in affected connectors
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for or manipulate market conditions causing position to go underwater
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: TVLHelper.getTVL reverts, blocking all vault operations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Vault is permanently frozen until position is manually resolved (if possible)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Market conditions alone can trigger this naturally

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All deposits, withdrawals, and fee calculations frozen
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires manually resolving the underwater position, which may require governance intervention
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: getTVL, all AccountingManager operations depending on TVL

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Market downturns or interest rate spikes

threats.title: UNIv3Connector TVL Queries Aggregated Pool Position Instead of Per-NFT, Overcounting TVL
threats.hypothesis_description: UNIv3Connector._getPositionTVL computes a position key as keccak256(abi.encodePacked(positionManager, tL, tU)) and queries pool.positions(key). This returns the TOTAL liquidity for ALL NonfungiblePositionManager NFTs at those ticks across all users, not just the vault's specific tokenId. The vault's TVL is inflated by external users' liquidity at the same tick range, leading to overvalued shares and enabling value extraction.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Uniswap V3 pool aggregates liquidity by owner address (positionManager) and tick range. The correct approach is to query positionManager.positions(tokenId) for the specific NFT's liquidity, not the pool-level aggregate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bytes32 key = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(positionManager, tL, tU)); pool.positions(key) returns aggregate liquidity for ALL NFTs at those ticks, not just the vault's tokenId

preconditions: Vault holds a Uniswap V3 position
Other users have positions through the same NonfungiblePositionManager at the same tick range

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: TVL for UNI V3 positions includes liquidity from other users, inflating share price
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin action required — overcounting is inherent
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always present when other positions exist at same ticks

operational_questions: How significant is the TVL inflation for typical UNI V3 positions?
Can an attacker amplify this by adding liquidity at the same ticks?
Does the overcount affect share price calculations?
validation_requirements: Compare pool.positions(key).liquidity vs positionManager.positions(tokenId).liquidity
Calculate the delta in TVL between aggregate and per-NFT values
Verify share price impact

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify vault with UNI V3 positions and check the inflated TVL
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: An attacker can add large liquidity at the same tick range through positionManager to further inflate
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Deposit into vault at inflated share price or withdraw with overcounted shares
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Remove external liquidity, vault TVL drops, remaining depositors bear the loss
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Capital for adding liquidity to the same pool/ticks

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Incorrect share pricing for all vault depositors
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract fix — cannot be patched operationally
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: TVL calculation, share minting/burning, fee calculations

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — single transaction to add liquidity at same ticks
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Knowledge of Uniswap V3 position mechanics

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large liquidity additions at same tick range as vault's position
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: TVL significantly higher than actual vault assets
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Deposits followed by rapid withdrawals after TVL spike

threats.title: Disabled Swap/Bridge Routes Remain Executable Due to onlyExistingRoute Modifier Bug
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyExistingRoute modifier in SwapAndBridgeHandler only reverts when `routes[_routeId].route == address(0) && !routes[_routeId].isEnabled`. Since actual routes have non-zero addresses, a disabled route (isEnabled=false, route=non-zero) passes this check. The emergency disabling mechanism via setEnableRoute is ineffective: disabled routes can still execute swaps and bridges.
threats.investigation_rationale: The condition should use OR (||) to block routes that are EITHER address(0) OR disabled. The current AND (&&) means both conditions must be true to revert. Neither executeSwap nor executeBridge independently check isEnabled.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (routes[_routeId].route == address(0) && !routes[_routeId].isEnabled) revert RouteNotFound(); — uses AND, should use OR

preconditions: A route has been added with a non-zero address
The route has been disabled via setEnableRoute(routeId, false)
An eligible user attempts to use the disabled route

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Emergency route disabling fails to prevent usage
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Maintainer/emergency role correctly calls setEnableRoute but the disable is ineffective
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — disabled routes remain usable indefinitely

operational_questions: Are there any disabled routes in production?
Has any route been disabled due to compromise or deprecation?
Could a compromised route be used to drain connector funds?
validation_requirements: Disable a route via setEnableRoute(id, false)
Verify executeSwap still works with the disabled route
Confirm the only guard is onlyExistingRoute modifier

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for or trigger route disabling (e.g., compromised LiFi endpoint)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Continue using the disabled route for swaps/bridges
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute potentially unsafe swaps through the supposedly disabled route
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Drain funds via the compromised route that administrators believed was disabled
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Eligible user access (connector or other authorized address)

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Emergency route disabling mechanism is entirely bypassed
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Compromised routes cannot be effectively shut down
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade or workaround (e.g., setting route address to 0)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Swap executions through routes with isEnabled=false

threats.title: DolomiteConnector.openBorrowPosition Incorrectly Passes removePosition=true, Losing Position Tracking
threats.hypothesis_description: DolomiteConnector.openBorrowPosition calls registry.updateHoldingPosition with the last argument as `true` (remove) instead of `false` (add). This means opening a borrow position either removes an existing holding position or does nothing (returns type(uint256).max if position doesn't exist yet). Dolomite borrow positions are never properly tracked in the registry, leading to incorrect TVL calculations and untracked debt exposure.
threats.investigation_rationale: The boolean parameter naming convention throughout the codebase uses `true` for removePosition. The deposit function in the same contract correctly uses `false`. This is a clear copy-paste or logic error.

proof_of_signal.pattern: registry.updateHoldingPosition(vaultId, ..., abi.encode(accountId), '', true); — last parameter `true` means remove, but function is opening/adding a position

preconditions: Keeper calls openBorrowPosition on DolomiteConnector
Vault has a deposit position tracked in registry

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Opening a borrow position may accidentally remove the deposit position tracking
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Normal keeper strategy execution triggers this

operational_questions: Does the existing deposit position get removed when openBorrowPosition is called?
Are Dolomite borrow positions reflected in TVL calculations at all?
Can this lead to underreported debt and inflated share prices?
validation_requirements: Call deposit then openBorrowPosition and verify registry state
Check if TVL includes the borrow position's debt
Verify _getPositionTVL accuracy after openBorrowPosition

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Keeper deposits to Dolomite and opens a borrow position
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: The deposit tracking is removed from registry by the openBorrowPosition call
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: TVL calculation misses the position entirely or miscalculates
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Share price is incorrect, allowing value extraction

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Incorrect TVL calculations for Dolomite positions

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Holding positions disappearing after openBorrowPosition calls

threats.title: SiloConnector TVL Calculation Assumes 18 Decimals, Producing Incorrect Values for Non-18-Decimal Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: SiloConnector._getPositionTVL calculates price as `_getValue(assets[i], base, 1e18)` and then `depositAmount * price / 1e18`. This assumes all token balances are denominated in 18 decimal units. For 6-decimal tokens like USDC or USDT, the balance values are 1e12 times smaller than assumed, causing TVL to be dramatically underreported. Conversely, the debt subtraction may also be affected, potentially causing underflow.
threats.investigation_rationale: USDC and USDT are the most common base tokens and stablecoins used in DeFi. The project documentation confirms interaction with non-standard tokens. The deployment scripts show USDC as a base token.
threats.config_evidence: deployAccountingManager.ts shows baseTokenAddress = tokens['USDC'] which is 6 decimals

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 price = _getValue(assets[i], base, 1e18); totalDepositAmount += depositAmount * price / 1e18; — hardcoded 1e18 does not match token decimals for USDC (6), USDT (6), WBTC (8)

preconditions: Silo position involves tokens with non-18 decimals (USDC, USDT, WBTC)
TVL calculation is triggered

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: TVL for Silo positions with 6-decimal tokens will be incorrect by a factor of ~1e12

operational_questions: What tokens are used in Silo positions in production?
Does the TVL miscalculation lead to inflated or deflated share prices?
Can this be exploited for value extraction?
validation_requirements: Deploy Silo position with USDC (6 decimals) and calculate TVL
Compare with expected TVL using correct decimal handling
Verify share price impact

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify vault with Silo positions using USDC or USDT
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: TVL calculation produces incorrect values due to decimal mismatch
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Share price is mispriced, enabling arbitrage
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Deposit when underpriced, withdraw when correctly priced (or vice versa)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Incorrect share pricing for all vault depositors

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: TVL values for Silo positions that don't match expected values

threats.title: SwapAndBridgeHandler Has No Function to Remove Eligible Users, Creating Irrevocable Access
threats.hypothesis_description: The SwapAndBridgeHandler contract has addEligibleUser but no corresponding removeEligibleUser function. Once an address is granted swap/bridge execution rights via addEligibleUser, this access can never be revoked. If a connector is decommissioned or compromised, its swap/bridge privileges persist indefinitely.
threats.investigation_rationale: The isEligibleToUse mapping is only set to true via addEligibleUser and the constructor. There is no setter to false. All connector addresses added as eligible users retain permanent access.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function addEligibleUser(address _user) external onlyMaintainerOrEmergency { isEligibleToUse[_user] = true; } — no corresponding remove function exists

preconditions: An address has been added as eligible user
The address needs to be revoked (decommissioned connector, compromised address)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Decommissioned connectors retain swap/bridge execution privileges
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Maintainer/emergency cannot revoke eligible user status

operational_questions: How many eligible users have been added in production?
Are any eligible users decommissioned connectors?
Can an eligible user abuse swap/bridge access after being decommissioned?
validation_requirements: Verify no removeEligibleUser function exists
Test that a decommissioned connector can still execute swaps

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Eligible user address is decommissioned or compromised
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute unauthorized swaps or bridges using retained privileges
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No administrative mechanism to revoke access
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Access to a previously eligible address

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After eligible user should be revoked

threats.title: SNXv3Connector Double-Counts Collateral in TVL Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: SNXV3Connector._getPositionTVL computes TVL as `_getValue(collateralType, base, totalDeposited + totalAssigned)`. In SNX V3, `totalDeposited` already includes `totalAssigned` (delegated collateral) and `totalLocked`. Adding `totalAssigned` again double-counts the delegated portion, inflating the reported TVL.
threats.investigation_rationale: SNX V3 getAccountCollateral returns (totalDeposited, totalAssigned, totalLocked) where totalDeposited >= totalAssigned >= totalLocked. The implementation adds totalDeposited + totalAssigned, double-counting the assigned portion.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (uint256 totalDeposited, uint256 totalAssigned, uint256 totalLocked) = SNXCoreProxy.getAccountCollateral(...); tvl = _getValue(collateralType, base, totalDeposited + totalAssigned); — totalDeposited already includes totalAssigned

preconditions: SNX V3 position exists with delegated collateral (totalAssigned > 0)
TVL calculation is triggered

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: TVL for SNX positions inflated by the delegated amount
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Normal keeper operations for delegation trigger this
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always present when collateral is delegated

operational_questions: Does SNX V3 getAccountCollateral return totalDeposited inclusive of totalAssigned?
What percentage inflation does this cause in practice?
Can an attacker exploit the inflated TVL for share price manipulation?
validation_requirements: Query getAccountCollateral for a position with delegated collateral
Verify totalDeposited >= totalAssigned
Calculate TVL overcount

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Vault has SNX position with delegated collateral
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: TVL is inflated by totalAssigned (counted twice)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Deposit at fair price, delegate to inflate, withdraw at inflated price

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of SNX V3 collateral model

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: TVL higher than actual collateral value

threats.title: Global flashLoan Address Grants Cross-Vault Manager Access via BalancerFlashLoan
threats.hypothesis_description: The registry.flashLoan() is a single global address that passes the onlyManager modifier for ALL vaults. The BalancerFlashLoan contract only validates that the original caller is the keeper for the decoded vaultId, but the actual destinationConnector calls execute with msg.sender=BalancerFlashLoan, which passes onlyManager for any connector regardless of vault. A keeper for vault A can use flash loans to call manager-restricted functions on vault B's connectors.
threats.investigation_rationale: NoyaGovernanceBase.onlyManager checks `msg.sender == registry.flashLoan()` as a universal pass. receiveFlashLoan validates caller against one vaultId but destinationConnector targets are unvalidated. This creates cross-vault privilege escalation.
threats.config_evidence: deploySharedContracts.ts shows registry deployed with flashLoan=address(0), but setFlashLoanAddress exists to set it

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (!(msg.sender == keeperContract || msg.sender == emergencyManager || msg.sender == registry.flashLoan())) revert — flashLoan is global, not per-vault

preconditions: BalancerFlashLoan contract is deployed and set as registry.flashLoan()
Multiple vaults exist with different keepers
Vault A's keeper is compromised or colluding

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Flash loan contract acts as universal manager for all vaults
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Flash loan address must be set in registry (MAINTAINER_ROLE)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent once flashLoan address is set

operational_questions: Is the BalancerFlashLoan set as registry.flashLoan() in production?
Can vault A's keeper use flash loans to interact with vault B's connectors?
Are destinationConnector calls scoped to the requesting vault?
validation_requirements: Verify that BalancerFlashLoan can call onlyManager functions on any connector
Test cross-vault access via flash loan mechanism
Confirm destinationConnector validation gap

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Vault A's keeper initiates flash loan with vault A's vaultId in userData
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: receiveFlashLoan validates caller as vault A's keeper ✓
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: destinationConnector calls target vault B's connectors
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Calls pass onlyManager because msg.sender == registry.flashLoan()
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Control of any vault's keeper multi-sig

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete cross-vault access from any single vault's keeper
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Arbitrary manager operations on any vault's connectors
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires architecture redesign for per-vault flash loan scoping
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All onlyManager-gated functions across all connectors

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: High — requires keeper multi-sig compromise for one vault
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Advanced — understanding of cross-vault flash loan mechanism
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Anytime after flash loan is configured

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Flash loan calls targeting connectors from different vaults
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Position changes in vaults not initiated by their keepers

threats.title: BalancerFlashLoan Allows Arbitrary External Calls via Unvalidated destinationConnector Array
threats.hypothesis_description: In BalancerFlashLoan.receiveFlashLoan, the `destinationConnector` addresses decoded from userData are called with arbitrary calldata without validating them as active connectors. Only the `receiver` address (where tokens are sent) is validated. The keeper multi-sig can execute arbitrary calls to any contract (not just registered connectors) through the flash loan mechanism, bypassing connector-specific safety checks.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code validates `registry.isAnActiveConnector(vaultId, receiver)` but then calls `destinationConnector[i].call{gas:gas[i]}(callingData[i])` without any validation that destinationConnector[i] is an active connector.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (bool success,) = destinationConnector[i].call{ value: 0, gas: gas[i] }(callingData[i]); — destinationConnector is not validated as active connector

preconditions: BalancerFlashLoan is deployed and set as registry.flashLoan()
Keeper multi-sig is compromised or colluding
Flash loan tokens are available

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Keeper can make arbitrary external calls to any address through flash loan
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Keeper multi-sig controls the flash loan execution

operational_questions: Can destinationConnector include non-connector addresses?
Could this be used to approve tokens to external addresses?
What damage can be done via arbitrary calls during a flash loan?
validation_requirements: Craft flash loan userData with non-connector destinationConnector
Verify the call executes successfully
Test token approval and extraction scenarios

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Compromised keeper crafts userData with malicious destinationConnector addresses
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Flash loan executes, sending tokens to legitimate receiver
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Arbitrary calls to non-connector addresses execute (e.g., token approvals, external protocol calls)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Extract value through the unauthorized interactions

potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All operations during flash loan execution

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Flash loan destinationConnector addresses that are not registry-approved
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected contract calls during flash loans
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Flash loan calls to non-standard targets

threats.title: Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity Is Empty, Providing Zero Withdrawal Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity has an empty function body. It is called by BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress during the accounting manager withdrawal path, intended to validate that withdrawal amounts are legitimate. Since it performs no verification, any withdrawal amount passes, eliminating the watcher safety layer that is described in the protocol documentation as a critical security control.
threats.investigation_rationale: The project documentation states 'Watchers: A monitoring role capable of undoing actions if misbehavior is detected.' The actual implementation is an empty function that provides zero protection. The Watchers contract inherits Keepers (a multi-sig) suggesting it should contain multi-sig-verified checks.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function verifyRemoveLiquidity(uint256 withdrawAmount, uint256 sentAmount, bytes memory data) external view { } — completely empty function body

preconditions: Accounting manager calls sendTokensToTrustedAddress on any connector
Withdrawal data contains any newAmount

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Watcher verification step is a no-op in the withdrawal path
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Accounting manager (trusted) controls withdrawal amounts without external check
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Every withdrawal through accounting manager path

operational_questions: Is this intentionally empty or was implementation missed?
Are there off-chain watcher mechanisms that compensate?
Could a compromised or buggy accounting manager exploit the missing check?
validation_requirements: Verify verifyRemoveLiquidity body is empty
Test withdrawal with arbitrary amounts through accounting manager path
Confirm no revert regardless of parameters

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Accounting manager initiates withdrawal via sendTokensToTrustedAddress
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: verifyRemoveLiquidity is called but performs no check
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Any withdrawal amount (including full connector balance) is approved
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires compromised accounting manager or keeper to exploit

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Watcher security layer is completely absent
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: No automated protection against suspicious withdrawals
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires implementing actual verification logic in Watchers contract
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All withdrawals through accounting manager path

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unusually large withdrawals without watcher intervention
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: All withdrawals succeeding regardless of amount
